subject: AP World History
theme: Between 1450 and 1750, European exploration and colonization created transoceanic connections that linked the Americas, Africa, Asia, and Europe for the first time, producing the Columbian Exchange, the Atlantic slave trade, and new global economic systems that reshaped societies worldwide.
core_concepts: European maritime exploration was driven by the desire for direct access to Asian luxury goods, the spread of Christianity, technological advances (compass, caravel, astrolabe), and competition among European states.

The Columbian Exchange transferred plants (potatoes, maize, tobacco), animals (horses, cattle), diseases (smallpox, measles), and peoples between the Eastern and Western Hemispheres, transforming environments, diets, and demographics worldwide.
European colonization of the Americas devastated indigenous populations through disease (up to 90% mortality), military conquest (Spanish conquistadors), and forced labor systems (encomienda, mita).
The Atlantic slave trade forcibly transported approximately 12 million Africans to the Americas between 1500 and 1866, creating a racialized labor system that enriched European colonial economies.
Mercantilism drove European colonial policy — colonies existed to enrich the mother country through the export of raw materials and the import of manufactured goods.
The triangular trade connected Europe (manufactured goods), Africa (enslaved people), and the Americas (raw materials) in a transatlantic commercial system.
Maritime empires (Portuguese, Spanish, Dutch, British, French) competed for control of trade routes and colonial territories, establishing plantation economies dependent on coerced labor.
The silver trade, fueled by Spanish mines at Potosi, created the first truly global economy by linking the Americas to Europe, Africa, and Asia through flows of precious metals.

key_terms.definition: Transfer of plants, animals, diseases, peoples, and cultures between the Eastern and Western Hemispheres following 1492.
Forced transportation of approximately 12 million Africans to the Americas (1500–1866) to provide labor for plantation economies.
Spanish colonial system granting settlers the right to demand tribute and forced labor from indigenous peoples in exchange for protection and Christian instruction.
Economic theory that national wealth depends on accumulating gold and silver through favorable trade balances, driving colonial extraction policies.
Atlantic trade pattern: European manufactured goods to Africa, enslaved Africans to the Americas, and colonial raw materials (sugar, tobacco, cotton) to Europe.
The horrific transatlantic voyage endured by enslaved Africans from the West African coast to the Americas, marked by extreme mortality.
Spanish military adventurers (Cortes, Pizarro) who conquered the Aztec and Inca empires using firearms, horses, steel, and allied indigenous peoples.
Massive silver mine in present-day Bolivia that produced enormous wealth for the Spanish Empire and fueled global silver trade.
Agricultural system based on large-scale cash crop production (sugar, tobacco, cotton) using enslaved or coerced labor.
Business organization (e.g., Dutch East India Company, British East India Company) that pooled investor capital to fund colonial and trade ventures.
Spanish trade route (1565–1815) connecting Manila (Philippines) to Acapulco (Mexico), exchanging Asian goods for American silver.
Small, maneuverable Portuguese sailing ship with lateen sails that enabled European exploration along African coasts and across the Atlantic.
Person of mixed European and indigenous American ancestry, reflecting the racial blending that occurred in Spanish colonial societies.
Hierarchical racial classification system in Spanish colonies that ranked people by racial ancestry, with peninsulares (Spanish-born) at the top.

exam_tip: College Board frequently tests the global effects of the Columbian Exchange and the silver trade — be prepared to trace how a specific exchange (e.g., potatoes to Europe, silver to China) affected demographics, economies, and environments in multiple regions.
